1: Psalm 39 is addressed to Jeduthun, one of the three chief Levitical musicians appointed by David (1 Chr 16:41-42; 25:1, 3, 6; 2 Chr 5:12). Jeduthun is associated with a musical style or tradition, though its specific character is lost. The psalm is one of the Psalter's most existentially searching meditations: it opens with the psalmist's resolution to silence and ends with a request that God look away — both the opposite of typical lament conventions. "I will keep watch over my ways (ʾešmĕrāh dĕrākay) so I will not sin with my tongue" — the restraint of speech is a wisdom discipline (Prov 13:3; 21:23; Jas 3:2); the specific concern here is not to sin verbally in the presence of the wicked. The muzzle metaphor ("muzzle over my mouth while the wicked are in front of me") — maḥsôm (muzzle, bridle) — is unique in the Psalter: the psalmist bridles his own mouth as one bridles an animal, preventing speech that might dishonor God or give the wicked an advantage.
2: "I was mute (neʾĕlamtî) and utterly silent (dûmiyyāh), holding back even from what was good (miṭṭôb), and my pain intensified (nĕʿkār kaʾăbî)" — the complete, self-imposed silence extends even to "good" speech — not just avoiding sinful complaints but suppressing even appropriate expression of suffering. Dûmiyyāh (silence, stillness) is the deepest form of silence (also Ps 62:1: "For God alone my soul waits in silence"). The paradox: the discipline of silence intended to prevent sin actually intensified the pain (kaʾab, to be in pain, to ache). Suppression of suffering does not relieve it; the psalmist discovers that silence was not the answer. The resolution comes in v. 3 when the inner fire finally breaks out in speech.
3: "My heart grew hot (ḥam-libbî) within me; as I brooded (bĕhagîgî), the fire blazed up; then I spoke (dibbartî) with my tongue" — three stages: heating, conflagration, eruption into speech. Hāgāh (to brood, to meditate, to mutter) — the inward meditation of the Psalter (Ps 1:2; 37:30; 77:12) here produces heat rather than comfort; the brooding intensifies the fire rather than cooling it. Jeremiah 20:9 describes the identical experience: "If I say 'I will not mention him or speak any more in his name,' his word is in my heart like a fire, a fire shut up in my bones. I am weary of holding it in; indeed, I cannot." The compulsive breaking of the silence — the fire that cannot be contained — is what produces the prayer of vv. 4-13.
4: "LORD, show me my end (qiṣṣî) — how long I have to live; let me grasp how short-lived I am (mah-ḥādēl ʾānî)" — an unusual prayer: the psalmist asks not for longer life but for clear knowledge of life's brevity. Qēṣ (end, limit) — the terminus of one's existence. Ḥādēl (fleeting, transient, that which ceases) — from ḥādal (to stop, to cease, to desist). The request is for the wisdom of mortality: Ps 90:12 prays "teach us to number our days so that we may gain a heart of wisdom." The ANE wisdom tradition (Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Israelite) frequently used meditation on death as the path to wise living. The psalmist doesn't want to avoid death but to grasp it so clearly that it reorients the present.
5: "You have made my days barely a handbreadth (ṭĕpāḥôṯ) long; my lifetime is as nothing in your sight. Truly every person at their best is only a breath (hebel). Selah" — ṭepah (handbreadth) — the smallest standard unit of Hebrew measurement, approximately 3 inches (7-8 cm), the width of four fingers. Human life measured in handbreadths before the eternal God is almost unmeasurably small. Hebel (breath, vapor, emptiness, vanity) — the central term of Ecclesiastes (Eccl 1:2: "vanity of vanities, all is vanity"; hakkōl hebel). The word occurs twelve times in Ecclesiastes alone and is the thematic organizing concept of that book. Here it appears twice (vv. 5, 11) — Psalm 39 and Ecclesiastes share the same mortality vocabulary and perspective. "Selah" — a liturgical pause, giving space for the weight of this observation to settle.
6: "Surely everyone moves about like a shadow (ṣelem); they are in commotion (yehĕmāyûn) for nothing; they pile up wealth and do not know who will collect it (yĕʾaspēm)" — three observations on the futility of hebel-existence. Ṣelem (shadow, image, likeness) is used in Gen 1:26-27 for humans as made "in the image (ṣelem) of God" — the identical word here reduced to a shadow in motion. The irony is sharp: the creature made in the divine image turns out to be a shadow. "Commotion for nothing" — the busy anxiety (hāmāh, to be in turmoil, to roar, to murmur) of human economic and social striving. "They pile up wealth and do not know who will collect it" — Eccl 2:18-21; 4:8; Luke 12:20 ("fool, tonight your soul is required of you, and the things you have prepared, whose will they be?"). The accumulation anxiety is universal and universally futile.
7: "And now, Lord, what do I wait for (mah-qiwwîṯî)? My hope (tôḥalṯî) is in you" — the pivot of the meditation: having established the utter transience of human life (vv. 4-6), the psalmist's question is "what then is worth waiting for?" The answer is not a what but a Who. Qāwāh (to wait, to hope with tension like a taut cord) and yāḥal (to hope, to wait with expectation) — both hope-words appear in close proximity, reinforcing that the only stable object of hope in a hebel-world is YHWH himself. This is the existential move of the Psalter against the futility perspective: not denial of transience (v. 5 is not retracted) but reorientation of hope from temporal possessions to the eternal God.
8: "Rescue me from all my transgressions (pĕšāʿay); do not make me a disgrace (ḥerpāh) before fools (nābāl)" — the petition moves from mortality-meditation back to the specific crisis of the opening verse. Pešaʿ (transgression, rebellion, covenant breach) is the broadest and most severe term for sin — deliberate defection. Nābāl (fool) is the wisdom tradition's term for the morally and practically bankrupt person — not intellectual but ethical: the one who acts as if God does not exist or does not matter (Ps 14:1: "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'"). The psalmist prays to be spared the shame of being a spectacle for fools — the double shame of being simultaneously a sinner and publicly humiliated before those who would use that humiliation to mock God.
9: "I am silent (neʾĕlamtî); I do not open my mouth (lōʾ ʾepṣeh-pî), because this is your doing (kî ʾattāh ʿāśîṯā)" — the silence of v. 2 returns, but transformed. In v. 2, the silence was strategic discipline; in v. 9, after the eruption of speech (vv. 3-12), the silence is theological acceptance: "because you have done this." The psalmist acknowledges YHWH's agency in his suffering — not mechanically (as if suffering were deserved), but covenantally: the God who acts in history has acted here, and the speaker accepts that. This is the Job-posture: "The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD" (Job 1:21).
10: "Take away (hāsēr) your affliction from me; I am worn down (kālîṯî) by the blow (tigrāṯ) of your hand" — having accepted divine agency (v. 9), the psalmist now petitions for relief. This is not contradiction but honest covenantal dialogue: acceptance of divine action does not preclude petition against it. Job exhibits the same pattern (Job 1:21 followed by Job 3:1). Kālāh (to be spent, to be completed, to be exhausted) describes the complete depletion of energy and resources (Ps 71:9; 102:3; Isa 40:28). "The blow (tigrāh) of your hand" — tigrāh is a hapax (unique occurrence), possibly related to gāraʿ (to diminish, to take away) or to a root meaning "blow, stroke." The tactile image of the divine hand striking the psalmist is the same as v. 2's "your hand has come down hard on me."
11: "When you correct (bĕtôkāḥôṯ) someone for their iniquity with rebukes, you consume like a moth (kāʿāš bālîṯā) everything they prize; truly every person is only a breath (hebel). Selah" — tôkēḥāh (correction, proof, rebuke) is the term for the wise person's authoritative rebuke that produces change (Prov 1:23, 25, 30; 3:11; 5:12; 10:17; 12:1; 13:18). "Consume like a moth" — the moth as destroyer of precious garments (Isa 51:8; Job 13:28; Matt 6:19; Jas 5:2) is the image of silent, invisible, gradual consumption that reduces what was prized to nothing. The moth-consumed garment ends as hebel. Second "Selah" — the second pause around the hebel statement reinforces its weight in the psalm's structure.
12: "Hear my prayer (šimʿāh ṯĕpillāṯî), LORD, and listen to my cry; do not stay silent (al-teḥĕraš) at my tears (dimeʿāṯî). I am a stranger (gēr) in your presence, a temporary traveler (tôšāb), as all my ancestors were" — the legal pair gēr wĕtôšāb (resident alien and temporary sojourner) is the standard OT designation for those with no land-rights in the community (Lev 25:23, 35, 47; Gen 23:4; 1 Chr 29:15). Abraham used this self-description before the Hittites (Gen 23:4); Isaac is a sojourner (Gen 26:3); the patriarchs generally "sojourned" rather than owning the promised land (Heb 11:13-16 takes this up explicitly: "they admitted that they were strangers and exiles on the earth"). To be a gēr before YHWH is to acknowledge that one's very presence in the covenant community is a gracious gift, not an inherent right. The appeal to ancestors connects the psalmist to the whole patriarchal tradition of dependent sojourning.
13: "Turn your gaze (tašaʿ) from me, so that I may recover joy (wĕʾabĕlîgāh) before I depart and am gone (ʾênenî)" — the most paradoxical petition of the entire psalm: rather than the usual "look at me, help me," the psalmist asks YHWH to look away. Šāʿāh (to look at, to gaze upon) — divine looking is experienced not as comfort but as intensifying scrutiny, like the divine gaze in Job 7:17-19: "What is man that you make so much of him... examine him every morning and test him every moment? Will you never look away from me?" The request for a moment's relief from divine attention — "so that I may recover joy before I die" — is the most honest expression of the human experience of divine scrutiny in the Psalter. The psalm that opened with voluntary silence ends with a request for a moment of unseen-ness: brief relief, a breath of freedom before the inevitable end.
